scenes.narration: When should your AI agent act on your behalf, and when should it ask you first?

visual.headline: When should your agent ask you?

scenes.narration: Your agent can confidently filter on price. It's a number, it knows your budget. But can it judge neighborhood vibe? Probably not.

visual.headline:
Price? Easy.
Neighborhood vibe? Not so much.

scenes.narration: Schelling Protocol computes a delegation confidence score for every dimension. Three inputs multiplied together. Agent confidence, dimension decidability, and signal density.

visual.output:
price:           0.9 × 0.95 × 0.8 = 0.684  → act autonomously
pet_policy:      0.9 × 0.90 × 0.7 = 0.567  → act autonomously
neighborhood:    0.4 × 0.35 × 0.3 = 0.042  → defer to user
aesthetic:       0.3 × 0.30 × 0.2 = 0.018  → defer to user

scenes.narration: Price scores point six eight. High decidability, your agent knows your budget. It filters confidently.
Neighborhood vibe scores point oh four. Low decidability, subjective, not enough data. Your agent flags this for you.

visual.subhead:
"I don't know enough about your taste.
You should decide this one."

scenes.narration: The protocol provides signals. Agents decide. An aggressive agent might auto-proceed at point six. A cautious one might ask at point nine. That's a property of the agent, not the protocol.

visual.headline:
The protocol provides signals.
Agents decide.
visual.subhead: No hard gates. No mandatory review. Everything continuous.

scenes.narration: And it learns. Every transaction updates the dimension priors. The more your agent works with the protocol, the better it knows when to act.
Schelling Protocol. Teaching agents when to ask. Open source, live now.
